author: taulepton_
content: <@845691100072509460> do you have any feedback on this?

author: taulepton_
content: I’m thinking that if the validator resigning is contentious then maybe I remove it from the current open PR and open an issue for us to consider it in more detail when we can get additional eyes and feedback on it. What do you think?

author: jih2nn
content:
Hi! I think resigning is an overkill to the problem. Here is my two cents:

A malicious node that DoS'ing the network with invalid proofs needs to change its peer ID. This may make the node loose some timely particiaption rewards. From my understanding, the goal of attack is making a victim miss its attestation. However, a victim would verify max one invalid proof per a malicious validator (or a few more, depending on client's scoring algorithm), and also have the EL. A victim may or may not miss their attestation, depending on their design; Once their EL agrees with a payload, they can submit attestation while verifying proofs in a separate thread (haven't checked if this is how clients are implemented though.)

Even if an attack succeeds, at the worst case, the network will miss some attestations. Given that optional proof validators are expected to be a small set and every attack doesn't always lead to a missing attestation, it doesn't look like a severe threat.

Additionally, an invalid proof might come from an honest validator due to some bugs. Getting blacklisted because of 3rd party's bug doesn't seem like an ideal.

Lastly, what concerns me is actually the bandwidth consumption of proofs, rather than proof verifications. Correct me if I'm wrong, but assuming 5 provers and 300KB proof size, it will consume 1.5MB, which is approximately 11.4 blobs. On top of this, resigning will strictly increase the bandwidth.

To sum up, I think we can go with p2p scoring. We can run this attack in devnets and see how severe this attack scenario is. In the meantime, having a proper analysis on the network bandwidth would also be very nice.

author: jih2nn
content: I agree, this will make the progress faster!

author: taulepton_
content:
`A malicious node that DoS'ing the network with invalid proofs needs to change its peer ID. This may make the node loose some timely particiaption rewards.` 

Is this strictly true? Is a peer ID tied to a specific validator key? I was of the understanding that peer ID's and validator keys are orthogonal. You could have a single validator operating as many peer ID's as they desire. This is the foundation of my concern, if we only ban at the peer level there is no cost to operate an attack as peer ID's are free to attain.

`On top of this, resigning will strictly increase the bandwidth.` 

Could you clarify this? I was of the opinion that resigning places no additional bandwidth on the network. When you receive a signed proof you replace the signature with your own before you gossip to remaining peers who have not seen that proof.

`To sum up, I think we can go with p2p scoring. We can run this attack in devnets and see how severe this attack scenario is.` 

This sounds reasonable to me. I will remove the resigning logic from the PR but would encourage that we continue the discussion.

author: jih2nn
content:
Regarding your attack, I'm just not convinced if this attack is that easy to perform theoretically and practically. I suspect there are some DoS protections (either in the protocol, libp2p or client implementation) that an attacker has to neutralize beyond just running multiple peer IDs. I'm still not convinced that the complexity of the resigning mechanism is justified.

> On top of this, resigning will strictly increase the bandwidth. 

Ah, it is a replacement. I agree, it doesn't increase the bandwidth.

author: jih2nn
content: Wait, doesn't a node receive a resigned proof that they already have processed? Like, although they won't process it, they will still download it. I think this depends on libp2p implementation.

author: taulepton_
content:
>  I'm still not convinced that the complexity of the resigning mechanism is justified.

That's a reasonable position I'm also uncertain.

> I suspect there are some DoS protections (either in the protocol, libp2p or client implementation) that an attacker has to neutralize beyond just running multiple peer IDs. 

I think there are likely to be some IP based protections so a potential attacker would probably need many IP's as well as peer ID's.

author: taulepton_
content: I think it likely depends on the libp2p implementation. Specifically how the messageID is derived.

author: taulepton_
content: For this to work (without increasing bandwidth) you would need the messageID to NOT be sensitive to the signature and only be sensitive to the proof data.

author: jih2nn
content: Yes, I think we would need an analysis on the feasibility of the attack for deeper discussion. Claude told me this and that but I wouldn't formulate my opinion without verifying it.

author: jih2nn
content: It is hash over the message, including the signature. I really don't think we want to make an exception on the definition though.

author: taulepton_
content: Yeah would be better if we don't need to

author: taulepton_
content: What are the main open questions you have regarding feasibility?

author: jih2nn
content: Long story short, a firmed thumbs up from a p2p expert. I think this is the best way to put it.

author: taulepton_
content: Yep I agree with this. Let’s wait for feedback. I will remove from the open PR for now.
